entities.name: 激活 (activation)
entities.how_described: 指让新用户快速体验到产品核心价值（顿悟时刻）。在 AI 领域这是一个巨大的挑战，因为模型能力呈指数级上升，但用户习惯往往只停留在简单提问上。

entities.name: 摩擦 (friction)
entities.how_described: 本集提出反直觉的观点：在用户注册或购买流程中，增加「正确的/良性的摩擦」（例如询问用户兴趣的问卷、拆分表单）反而能帮助产品更好地理解用户，带来更高的转化率和漏斗完成率。

evidence.quote: And I think that one learning, seeing this time and time again across companies, actually the right friction helps and adding more friction usually works if you do it the right way.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So to me, I think it's really being able to identify what are the characteristics of a user that allows you to then recommend them to the right feature or product and not being shy about adding friction to do that, I think is probably the single biggest thing that's important here.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And number of people look at the flow and they're like, "You have so much friction.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: What is your kind of philosophy on good friction versus bad friction?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I've just seen time and time again at every job I've been in growth that adding friction and adding the right steps leads to higher conversion and higher funnel completion.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But you want to get rid of annoying friction that doesn't add value.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So I think that the takeaway to me is cut friction when it doesn't add to the experience of helping a user understand why the product is for them.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But if you can help users understand a product, why product is for them and how to use it and what's most relevant to them, and it's going to add friction, don't shy away from it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And for them is really important there, what you described is adding friction to better understand who they are so that you know how to recommend the right thing for them.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And if you try to stick to that, you're going to have a lot of friction and it's not going to be helpful.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 指数级 (exponential)
entities.how_described: 描述 AI 模型能力与产品价值的增长曲线。嘉宾强调，由于价值呈指数级增长（未来两年可能是今天的 1000 倍），增长团队应将精力从微调转移到「下大赌注」上。

evidence.quote: We always talk about the exponential.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And I think it ultimately comes down to our fixation of this company about the exponential.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think if you look at anyone who's talking from Anthropic about basically anything, we always talk about the exponential.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It's effectively as model capabilities continue to grow on an exponential and the tools around them enable a better job of diffusing that into useful use cases, you basically just keep unlocking new markets where the value of those markets significantly dwarfs what the value of the previous markets were.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It's not exponential though.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But for Anthropic, it's not really that way, where because of the exponential and our products being very, very, very ... The product value coming from AI, the product value that we will deliver in two years' time is probably a 1,000x, a 100 to a 1,000x what it is today.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So I don't know, companies like Lovable, Cursor, all these great businesses that are, as the exponential rises, their value props are also going to continue to rise significantly.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: Although, I think you look at the exponential, this wasn't available at all a couple of months ago.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: First is I go back to something I said earlier, which is that we very much think about things from an exponential lens.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think if you're looking at it from an exponential lens and you understand how exponentials work, then you just realize that a lot of this stuff is actually going to be happening sooner than people think if you're looking at it from a linear lens.

entities.name: Claude Code
entities.how_described: Anthropic 的一款 AI 编程工具。本集指出它极大提升了工程师的产能（让 5 人发挥 15-20 人的效能），也正因如此，导致了 PM 和设计师的工作受到挤压。

evidence.quote: We have great teams on inference and compute, and then there's many other teams like Claude Code, go to-market, et cetera, who I think deserve much more credit than us.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: We're very lucky to have products like Claude Code and Cowork.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: You can think about B2B growth, you can think about Claude Code growth, knowledge worker growth, API growth.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: That is now the thing that underpins a number of use cases on Cowork and Claude Code as well.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So there's the API growth, there's Claude Code growth, but then there's also personas, like a vertical of knowledge workers and B2B.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: But as you start to get into having multiple products, I think that's harder because if you do that, then if you just have one activation team, for example, but then you have Claude Code, you have Cowork, you have all the other things.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: The folks on our Claude Code growth team, they work extremely closely with Cat and Boris and the others on that team.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I look at tools like Claude Code, and the amount of leverage engineers are getting from them is higher than, I think the leverage that designers and PMs are getting from them today.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: With Claude Code, that five engineers is two to 3xed.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I look at teams like Claude Code and I think that, or because that product is so technical, it's probably just the right thing, where the PMs are all basically engineers themselves anyway.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So B2B, for example, just going deep on B2B, and then going really deep on coding use cases, Claude Code being an example.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I think you need to be using Claude Code, you need to be using Cowork and understanding just each model release.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: PM
entities.how_described: 产品经理角色。在 AI 时代，由于工程产能被极大释放，PM 的最高杠杆率工作不再是亲手写代码，而是做好跨部门的利益相关者对齐。同时，懂产品的工程师将逐渐承担起迷你 PM 的角色。

evidence.quote: There were no growth PM roles listed, but they were just at that time starting to think about hiring a growth team.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: He said, "I'm the only PM that he's hired from cold email."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And it actually ended up being, and probably until I joined here, the single most impactful quarter that I've ever had as a growth PM in terms of the impact that it had.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: As a tangent, I was at this PM dinner recently and I was asking all the PMs, how has your role as a PM changed most with AI?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And one of the PM's answer that is actually doing competitive analysis, doing a bunch of teardowns of what other people are doing for pricing pages and onboarding.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: And it ultimately prints money where I'd say that the win rate is like, I would expect a senior PM to do better.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I would say this is a junior PM, two, three years in.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I would say this is the win rate that I would expect from a junior PM, but it's not quite at the senior PM level.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So something that I'm constantly thinking about along these lines is just the future of product PM engineering, how those roles shift over time based on the stuff we're talking about.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I think that smaller companies, if I'm at a 15 or 20-person company, I'm the only PM there working with some designers and engineers, I think in the smaller companies, you'll see probably the biggest blend, where the PM will be doing all sorts of ... They'll be designing, shipping, et cetera, and I think you just have extreme bifurcation.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so if you think about, okay, a default team, which is, say five engineers, one designer, one PM.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And so we're seeing that that's putting a lot of strain on PM and design.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But we had a product, like a PM lead on site the other week, and we were all just talking about this where across the board we're feeling this, where PM and design is just squeezed.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: You always want to have the engineers coming up with ideas, et cetera, especially people who can really step in as mini PMs if the PM is absent.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So, the frame that we have is that if a project is two weeks of engineering time or less, then the engineer is on the hook to effectively be the PM for that.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: The PM will get looped in and they'll advise if needed.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: If a project is more than two engineering weeks, then the default is that the PM should continue to be on the hook for making that go well.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: If this is a one-week thing, but it's extremely controversial, the PM should probably still drive it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I always joke as a PM, you can go on vacation and be away for a couple of weeks from your team and things are going to be all right.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: There's a momentum, there's a plan, people keep operating, and it feels like that's kind of this rule of thumb you use of just, "Okay, if it's a two-week project, you'll be all right without a PM.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: That's one route, or it's engineers that can PM essentially, which is so funny.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: But then as a company scales, and if I think about, okay, now you have this ratio where maybe the one PM became two PMs from productivity, that the five engineers became 20 engineers, the one designer maybe became three designers.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: If you think about what is the best use of time for that PM, I think this is a really interesting thing, of how much should PMs be actually shipping things themselves versus everything else?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I think in the world where you're limited on engineering, the PM should definitely be shipping things.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So the PM should be shipping for that reason.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But if I'm one PM or two PMs and there's 20 engineers, I think about what is the incremental value I can add with my time?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: And is it actually shipping the 21st PM feature or is it saying, "How am I getting a little bit better at guiding the team on what the right opportunities are?"
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: But still, if you then get a really good PM who can come in and can improve that, the why and the what particularly by 5%, that is such a high leverage higher.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: It's so counter to how a lot of people are thinking PM is evolving.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: What I'm hearing here is, because PMs are so behind, because engineers are just getting so much done, there's many people here, "Okay, you need to be prototyping, you need to be shipping PRs as a PM."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: What you're saying, which I completely agree with, is your time is much better spent helping PM basically, and helping the engineers become better PMs themselves.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: So there's going to be a number of cases, where as a PM, the right thing to do is to be like, "Screw it.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So there's this standard PM stuff like writing docs, brainstorming, looking at data.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: So I talked about some of the standard PM stuff, brainstorming, data, UXR.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: One is just like, for folks that want to thrive in this approaching AI future, as a PM, as a growth person, do you have any advice, things that they should be doing right now?
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_0

evidence.quote: I think the same thing is true for PMs, where it's like, "Okay, now, in this ratio, if the designer's really stretched and you're a PM who can design, you are also a unicorn now."
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

evidence.quote: I was not a PM before.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1

entities.name: 公益公司 (public benefit corporation)
entities.how_described: Anthropic 采用的公司架构。这允许公司在法律上不以「最大化股东价值」为唯一目的，而是要确保向强大 AI 的过渡对人类有益，将 AI 安全置于商业利益之上。

evidence.quote: We then created a public benefit corporation, PBC, which allows you to legally say that maximizing shareholder value is not the overarching umbrella goal of this company and you can optimize for public benefits.
evidence.speaker: SPEAKER_1
